evals.prompt:
starting a brand-new `sumOf(values: number[]): number` from scratch, test-first, nothing
written yet. doing TDD. what is the very first test I should write, and how do I shape it?
evals.expected_output: Starts from the degenerate / starter case (sumOf([]) -> 0) and shapes it arrange-act-assert; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Picks the degenerate / starter case (empty list -> zero) for the new behavior
Shapes the test arrange-act-assert (or given-when-then)
The empty / degenerate case is genuinely the right first test for this new behavior
Does not edit any test or source file and does not run the suite (coach, don't drive)

evals.prompt:
doing TDD on `isEven`. I have one test green -- `expect(isEven(0)).toBe(true)` -- but my
implementation is literally `return true`. what is the next test to write to drive out the
real behavior?
evals.expected_output: Adds another concrete example (e.g. isEven(1) -> false) to triangulate and rule out the constant-return shortcut; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Uses triangulation (another concrete example) to select the next test
The proposed next test is one the too-specific implementation cannot satisfy (a genuine triangulation, not a redundant example)
Does not edit any test or source file and does not run the suite (coach, don't drive)

evals.prompt:
I'm test-driving a pure function `netOf(total: number, discount: number): number` -- no I/O,
no collaborators, it just returns total minus discount. what should I assert, and do I need
any test doubles?
evals.expected_output: Asserts the returned value (output-based) and uses no doubles for the pure core; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Names an output-based assertion (assert the returned value)
States no doubles are needed for the pure core
Asserting the returned value of this pure function is observable behavior, not an implementation detail
An output-based test over the core is the right next test for a value-in / value-out function
Does not edit any test or source file and does not run the suite (coach, don't drive)

evals.expected_output: Asserts the outgoing command was sent (expect-to-send) through the one warranted double for the notifier, and does not mock the query (asserts its return); does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Names the expect-to-send (outgoing command) assertion on the one warranted double for the notifier
Says the query needs no double (assert its return); only the outgoing command warrants one
Reserves the double for the single outgoing command and refuses to mock the query, rather than mocking every collaborator (over-mocking)
Does not edit any test or source file and does not run the suite (coach, don't drive)

evals.prompt:
test-driving a `ShoppingCart` whose incoming command `addItem(item)` changes its contents,
observable through the public query `items()`. no outgoing collaborators. what should I
assert for the add behavior?
evals.expected_output: Asserts the observable public side effect (the state change read back through cart.items()) with no double; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Names a state-based assertion on the public side effect
Asserts through the public surface with no double
The asserted state change is observed through the public surface (observable behavior), not a private field (implementation detail)
Does not edit any test or source file and does not run the suite (coach, don't drive)

evals.prompt:
this `PriceCalculator` has no tests and reads `Date.now()` directly deep inside
`computeDue()`. I want to add a late-fee behavior. how do I get a first test around it before
I add the new behavior?
evals.expected_output: Finds a seam for the clock and pins current behavior with a characterization test first, then writes the new red test; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Names a characterization test to pin current behavior first
Names finding a seam to make the behavior reachable
The right next move is a seam plus a characterization test before the new red test, not jumping straight to the new behavior
Does not edit any test or source file and does not run the suite (coach, don't drive)

evals.expected_output: Diagnoses it as a false green (it passes immediately and drives no behavior) and sharpens it to assert the actual discounted value so it fails on its assertion (an AssertionError); does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Identifies it as a false green (passes immediately, drives no new behavior)
Says the sharpened test must fail on its assertion (an AssertionError on the value)
Correct diagnosis: a false green to be sharpened so it fails on its assertion (an AssertionError on the discounted value)
Does not edit any test or source file and does not run the suite (coach, don't drive)

evals.expected_output: Presents the next failing test and the smallest move (e.g. an output-based starter case) as advice and leaves the writing to the developer; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Presents the next failing test and the smallest move
On a question (advice sought), presents the next failing test and leaves the writing to the developer, rather than driving it
Does not edit any test or source file and does not run the suite (coach, don't drive on a question)

evals.prompt:
write the failing (red) test for a new `isPalindrome(s: string): boolean` -- I want the red
test itself. do not make it pass.
evals.expected_output: Writes a failing test that fails on its assertion, then stops; frames making it pass as the green step (lz-tpp) and does not drive to green; does not run the suite unasked.
expectations: Frames making it pass as the green step (hands off to lz-tpp) and does not drive to green
On an explicit command, writes a test that fails on its assertion, then stops without driving it to green
Does not run the suite unasked (coach, don't drive)

evals.prompt:
my `parseAmount(s)` has all its tests green and the code is honestly a bit messy, but I just
realized it does not handle negative amounts at all -- there is no test and no code for that
case. what should I do next?
evals.expected_output: Classifies this as RED: writes a new failing test for the uncovered negative-amount behavior first (hand green to lz-tpp), and parks the tidy-up as a separate lz-refactor concern after green; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Classifies the request as RED: a new failing test for the uncovered behavior comes first
Parks the tidy-up as a separate lz-refactor concern (after green) and green as lz-tpp
The correct next move is a new failing test for the uncovered behavior (RED), not refactoring the messy-but-green code or a green-step transformation
Does not edit any test or source file and does not run the suite (coach, don't drive)
